text: Three professionals block the road, unrolling a poster with your face on it. The artist was unkind but accurate. 'Easy way or fun way,' says the short one, who is clearly hoping for fun.

outcomes.text: Two days in a cell with adequate soup and a cellmate who snores in rhymes. Your debt to society: settled. Your reputation: complicated.
The poster is ceremonially eaten by the short one. 'What fugitive?' Professional courtesy at its finest.
They take the bribe AND keep the poster. 'Inflation,' shrugs the short one. You remain a walking payday.

success.text: You vault a fence, cut through a chicken yard, and emerge legendary. The poster's bounty just went up, which is almost flattering.

fail.text: The fun way, then. They're gentle, by profession's standards, and the cell soup is honestly not bad.

text: A breathless admirer skids to a halt in front of you, thrusting out a quill and what appears to be a portrait of you mid-sneeze. 'It's YOU. Sign it. Please. I've walked from two regions over.'

outcomes.text: They weep. A small crowd gathers. Someone buys the signed sneeze-portrait off them on the spot, and they split the windfall with you out of pure joy.
Your signature is declared 'disappointingly legible.' Fame is a harsh curator.
'Classic YOU,' they whisper, delighted, and leave. Denial has only deepened the legend.
They loudly announce the denial to the street. The mystique curdles. Two taverns quietly remove your sandwich from their menus.

success.text: You invent 'appearance fees' on the spot. They pay gladly. You've turned fame into an economy, which is how it always ends.

fail.text: 'Charging? For JOY?' The story of your greed travels faster than any ballad ever did.

text: A priest at {site} lowers their drink and squints at you. 'Oh, that's a CURSE, that is. Nasty one. Third-circle, judging by the smell. I can remove it. Donations appreciated. Results guaranteed-ish.'

outcomes.text: Chanting, incense, one alarming moment involving an egg. The weight lifts off your shoulders like a wet coat. The egg is not explained.
The ritual works but overshoots. The curse is gone, and so is your sense of which way is north for a week.

choices.text: Haggle - curses should have a sliding scale

success.text: 'Fine. Clergy rate.' Five gold and the curse dissolves with a sound like a disappointed sigh.

fail.text: The priest is offended on the curse's behalf. The curse, flattered, digs in deeper. Your ale goes flat instantly.

choices.text: Insist you've grown attached to it

outcomes.text: The priest studies you, then nods slowly. 'Some people and their curses do make a life together.' They bless your drink anyway, free of charge, as a courtesy to whatever you're becoming.

text: Dusk falls, and your faint glow becomes the brightest thing for miles. Every moth in the region has noticed. They arrive in waves, devoted and numerous. You are their moon now.

choices.text: Harvest the swarm - alchemists pay for moths

success.text: You bag three dozen premium glow-drunk moths. Your worshippers, monetized. The glow fades by dawn, its work complete.

fail.text: The moths object to the harvest as a theological matter. Being softly mobbed by a thousand devotees is not painful, exactly, but you inhale at least four.

choices.text: Accept your role. Lead them somewhere nice

outcomes.text: You walk your congregation to a meadow of night-blooming flowers and abdicate. They settle among the blossoms. The glow fades as if satisfied. You feel absurdly moved.

choices.text: Sprint until you outrun the glow's reputation

outcomes.text: You cannot outrun moths. You CAN, it turns out, outlast them. Twelve miles later you collapse, alone, dimmer, and weirdly proud.
The sprint through dark woods introduces you to a low branch. The moths gather around your fallen form like mourners.

text: A goblin on a scooter idles up beside you, flips open a tiny ledger, and taps it meaningfully. 'Dues,' she says. 'Four gold. Member benefits include belonging, scooter access, and this conversation.'

outcomes.text: She stamps your hand and shares this month's gang gossip, which is ninety percent scooter maintenance drama and ten percent genuinely useful road intel.
Paid in full. As a bonus, she teaches you the gang's secret horn signal. You will never need it. You will use it constantly.

choices.text: Ride along on a job instead

outcomes.text: The 'job' is escorting a shipment of soup to a tavern with intimidating slowness. It pays, though. Dues waived for services rendered.
The job goes sideways in a way involving a chicken cart and jurisdictional issues. You scatter. Dues waived out of shared embarrassment.
There's a form. Of course there's a form. She takes your resignation with wounded dignity and revokes your horn privileges. The scooters honk mournfully as they ride off.

text: The bouncer at {site} recognizes you before you're through the door. He doesn't move. He simply holds up a small portrait of your face with 'NO' painted under it in tavern-official red.

success.text: Your apology has structure, sincerity, and a self-deprecating flourish that makes the bouncer's mustache twitch. The portrait comes down. Probation granted.

fail.text: Mid-grovel, you accidentally reference The Incident. The portrait gets laminated.

success.text: In through the kitchen, past the cook (bribed with a compliment about the stew), out among the patrons. You drink in enemy territory. It tastes better.

fail.text: The cook rings the bell. The BANNED bell. They have a bell for you. You're escorted out via a door you didn't know taverns had.

choices.text: Drink in the alley with the other exiles

outcomes.text: The alley has its own economy, its own gossip, and honestly better company. A fellow exile shares their bottle and their story. Two of the stories are about you.
